bytes.topic: Compute Models — VMs vs containers vs serverless vs edge, choosing the right one
bytes.title: Azure Compute Models — Picking VMs, Container Apps, Functions, or Edge Zones
bytes.body:
Azure exposes four compute paradigms, each with hard trade-offs.

**Virtual Machines (VMSS):** Full OS control, ideal for stateful workloads, legacy lift-and-shift, or anything requiring specific kernel configs. Scale horizontally via VM Scale Sets with custom autoscale rules. High ops burden — you own patching.

**AKS vs Container Apps:** AKS gives full Kubernetes control; Container Apps abstracts the control plane and uses KEDA under the hood for event-driven scaling. Choose Container Apps when you want microservices without managing node pools, upgrades, and PodDisruptionBudgets.

**Azure Functions (Flex Consumption):** The new Flex Consumption plan brings VNet integration and per-instance concurrency control — previously only on Premium. Cold starts remain the core penalty; use `WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE` with pre-warmed slots to blunt them. Best for short-lived, event-driven work: Blob triggers, Service Bus consumers, HTTP fan-outs.

**Edge Zones / Static Web Apps:** Azure Edge Zones colocate compute inside carrier facilities for sub-10ms latency. Static Web Apps distributes globally with integrated API routes backed by Functions.

Decision rule: compliance + stateful → VM; long-running services → AKS; event-driven, bursty → Functions; global low-latency UI or IoT → Edge.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Provision each compute tier via Azure CLI

# 1. VM Scale Set (general-purpose D-series)
az vmss create -g rg-prod -n app-vmss \
  --image Ubuntu2204 --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --instance-count 2 --lb app-lb

# 2. Container App (KEDA-backed, no cluster ops)
az containerapp create -g rg-prod -n api \
  --environment my-env --image myacr.azurecr.io/api:latest \
  --min-replicas 0 --max-replicas 10 \
  --scale-rule-name http-rule --scale-rule-type http \
  --scale-rule-http-concurrency 50

# 3. Function App — Flex Consumption with VNet
az functionapp create -g rg-prod -n fn-processor \
  --plan-name flex-plan --plan-sku FC1 \
  --storage-account stprod --vnet my-vnet --subnet fn-subnet

bytes.topic: Networking — VPCs, subnets, security groups, load balancers, DNS
bytes.title: Azure VNet Segmentation — Subnet Isolation with NSGs, Service Endpoints, and Private DNS
bytes.body:
Azure Virtual Networks use subnets for workload isolation, but NSGs alone leave gaps. Segment workloads into dedicated subnets — snet-app, snet-data, snet-mgmt — and attach NSGs at the subnet level, not the NIC, for cleaner audit trails. NSG rules are stateful and evaluated by ascending priority; always anchor a default-deny at priority 4096.

For PaaS access, Service Endpoints extend your VNet identity to Azure SQL or Storage firewalls — traffic stays on the Microsoft backbone without a public IP route. For stricter isolation, Private Endpoints assign a private IP directly inside your subnet and disable public access entirely. The catch: Private Endpoints require a paired Azure Private DNS Zone (e.g. privatelink.database.windows.net) linked to your VNet. Without it, DNS still resolves the public FQDN and connections silently fail.

In hub-spoke topologies, peer your spoke VNets to the hub and link all Private DNS Zones to the hub. Deploy an Azure Private DNS Resolver in the hub subnet so spoke VMs resolve privatelink records without custom DNS forwarders. Use Azure Firewall Premium in the hub to inspect east-west traffic between spokes via UDRs on each spoke subnet.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create VNet + segmented subnets with NSG and Service Endpoints
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-prod --resource-group rg-prod \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/16

az network nsg create --name nsg-data --resource-group rg-prod

az network vnet subnet create \
  --name snet-data --vnet-name vnet-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --address-prefix 10.0.2.0/24 \
  --network-security-group nsg-data \
  --service-endpoints Microsoft.Sql Microsoft.Storage

# Link Private DNS Zone for Azure SQL Private Endpoint
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name privatelink.database.windows.net

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group rg-prod --zone-name privatelink.database.windows.net \
  --name link-vnet-prod --virtual-network vnet-prod --registration-enabled false

bytes.topic: Storage — object vs block vs file storage, when to use each
bytes.title: Azure Storage — Object vs Block vs File: Picking the Right Service
bytes.body:
Azure offers three distinct storage primitives, each with a specific use case.

**Blob Storage (object)** — Use Azure Blob Storage for unstructured data: images, videos, backups, ML datasets. Access via REST or `BlobServiceClient`. Tiers (Hot/Cool/Archive) let you trade latency for cost. Enable static website hosting directly from a container.

**Managed Disks (block)** — Attach to Azure VMs as OS or data disks. Choose Premium SSD (P-series) for latency-sensitive workloads like databases, Ultra Disk for sub-millisecond I/O. Block storage is not accessible independently — it lives inside a VM.

**Azure Files (file)** — Fully managed SMB/NFS shares mountable on Windows, Linux, and macOS. Ideal for lift-and-shift apps that expect a file system, or sharing config/state across multiple App Service instances. Supports Azure AD Kerberos auth.

**Decision rule:** Serving assets or storing blobs → Blob Storage. VM disk or database volume → Managed Disk. Shared network drive or legacy app → Azure Files.

bytes.topic: Databases as a Service — managed DB offerings, read replicas, automated backups
bytes.title: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server — Read Replicas, Backups, and HA Explained
bytes.body:
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server is Azure's primary managed relational offering — no VM patching, built-in monitoring, and automated backups to Azure Blob Storage. Configure geo-redundant backups with 1–35 day retention at provision time; point-in-time restore (PITR) lets you fork a new Flexible Server from any second within that window using `az postgres flexible-server restore`.

Read replicas are full Flexible Server instances linked via streaming replication. Unlike RDS, there's no managed reader endpoint — route read traffic at the application layer by referencing the replica's FQDN directly. Replicas don't auto-failover; for automatic failover, use Zone-Redundant HA (`--high-availability ZoneRedundant`), which provisions a synchronous standby in a paired Availability Zone with ~60–120s RTO.

Key operational note: cross-region replicas incur egress costs. Monitor replication lag via `az postgres flexible-server replica list` and inspect the `replicationLag` field. For Azure SQL Managed Instance, read scale-out uses Always On Availability Groups with a built-in listener — a fundamentally different model than Flexible Server's manual routing approach.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a read replica in a secondary region
az postgres flexible-server replica create \
  --replica-name mydb-replica \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --source-server mydb-primary \
  --location westus2
# Point-in-time restore (forks a new server from any second in retention window)
az postgres flexible-server restore \
  --name mydb-pitr \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --source-server mydb-primary \
  --restore-time "2026-04-16T14:30:00Z"

bytes.topic: IAM & Permissions — least privilege, roles vs policies, service accounts, never root
bytes.title: Azure RBAC — Least Privilege with Managed Identities and Scoped Role Assignments
bytes.body:
Azure RBAC and Azure Policy solve different problems — RBAC controls who can do what, Policy enforces what configuration is allowed. Conflating them leads to gaps in both.

For service-to-service auth, always use Managed Identities (system-assigned or user-assigned) over service principals with client secrets. Secrets rotate poorly and leak; managed identities are token-vended via IMDS with no credential management. Your App Service, Container App, or AKS workload gets an AAD token automatically.

Scope role assignments as tightly as possible. The hierarchy is Management Group → Subscription → Resource Group → Resource. Assigning Contributor at subscription scope is Azure's equivalent of root. Prefer data-plane roles: Storage Blob Data Reader over Storage Account Contributor, Key Vault Secrets User over Key Vault Contributor. These grant only the surface your workload touches.

Never assign Owner or User Access Administrator to workload identities — both allow privilege escalation by granting roles to others. Reserve these for break-glass accounts protected by PIM with approval workflows and time-bound activation.

Audit regularly with az role assignment list --all and flag any Owner assignments outside your break-glass set.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a user-assigned managed identity
az identity create --name myapp-identity --resource-group myapp-rg

IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name myapp-identity --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --query principalId -o tsv)

STORAGE_SCOPE=$(az storage account show \
  --name mystorageacct --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Data-plane role scoped to the resource, NOT Contributor at RG level
az role assignment create \
  --assignee "$IDENTITY_ID" \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --scope "$STORAGE_SCOPE"

bytes.topic: Cost Optimization — reserved vs spot vs on-demand, rightsizing, budget alerts
bytes.title: Azure Cost Optimization — Reserved, Spot, On-Demand, and Rightsizing That Actually Saves Money
bytes.body:
Azure pricing has three compute tiers: On-Demand (pay-as-you-go, full price), Reserved Instances (1- or 3-year commitment, up to 72% off), and Spot VMs (up to 90% off, but evictable with 30-second notice). Use Reserved for predictable baseline workloads like App Services or always-on AKS node pools. Use Spot for batch jobs, CI/CD agents, and stateless workers — pair with Azure VMSS eviction policies and terminate handlers. On-Demand is only justified for unpredictable bursts or dev/test.

Rightsizing is where most Azure bills bleed. Use Azure Advisor's cost recommendations to catch over-provisioned VMs — it compares 7-day CPU/memory telemetry against SKU capacity. The `Az.Compute` PowerShell module or `az monitor metrics list` lets you pull this programmatically. Set Budget Alerts in Azure Cost Management with action groups to fire emails or Logic App webhooks at 80%/100% of threshold. Scope budgets to resource groups, not subscriptions, for team-level accountability. Tag resources with `costCenter` and `env` to slice spend in Cost Analysis.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Set a budget alert scoped to a resource group
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "team-api-monthly" \
  --amount 500 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-04-01 \
  --end-date 2027-04-01 \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-rg \
  --category Cost \
  --thresholds 80 100 \
  --contact-emails ops@example.com \
  --contact-roles Owner Contributor

bytes.topic: High Availability — multi-AZ deployments, failover, provider SLAs
bytes.title: Azure High Availability — Multi-Zone Deployments, Failover, and SLA Math
bytes.body:
Azure Availability Zones are physically separate datacenters within a single region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across 2+ zones upgrades your VM SLA from 99.9% (single instance) to 99.99% — a 40x reduction in allowed monthly downtime.

For compute, zone-redundant Azure Load Balancer (Standard SKU) distributes traffic across zones with no extra config. For AKS, pass `--zones 1 2 3` on node pools and pair with `topologySpreadConstraints` in pod specs to enforce spread — AKS alone won't guarantee it.

Storage: swap LRS for ZRS (`Standard_ZRS`) to replicate synchronously across 3 zones with zero RPO on zone failure. Azure SQL Database Business Critical tier places replicas in separate zones by default; General Purpose requires explicit zone-redundancy opt-in via the `zoneRedundant: true` property in Bicep.

Cross-region failover is a separate layer: Azure Front Door for active-active HTTP(S) with health probes, Azure Traffic Manager for DNS-based weighted/priority routing, and Azure Site Recovery for full VM replication to a paired region. Always check the `zones` property on your resource — many SKUs default to zone-pinned, not zone-redundant.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy an AKS node pool spread across all 3 availability zones
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKS \
  --name prodpool \
  --node-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --mode System

# Confirm zone assignment on the node pool
az aks nodepool show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKS \
  --name prodpool \
  --query "availabilityZones" -o tsv

bytes.topic: CDN & Edge — cache setup, invalidation, edge caching strategy
bytes.title: Azure Front Door — Cache Setup, Invalidation & Edge Strategy
bytes.body:
Azure Front Door Standard/Premium (AFD) replaces classic Azure CDN and is the right edge layer for any Azure workload. Cache behavior is configured per-route via cache settings on the AFD Route resource — not globally. Set `cacheBehavior` to `Override` and define `cacheDuration` (ISO 8601) to enforce TTL regardless of origin Cache-Control headers. For query strings, use `queryStringCachingBehavior: IgnoreQueryString` for static assets and `UseQueryString` for personalized or filtered API responses. Avoid `BypassCaching` unless the route is purely dynamic.

For cache invalidation, use `az afd endpoint purge` with specific content paths — wildcard purge (`/*`) nukes the entire edge cache globally across all POPs in seconds. Scope purges to changed paths to reduce blast radius. Pair this with versioned asset filenames (e.g., `app.a1b2c3.js`) to make cache busting deterministic and avoid purge dependency for static assets entirely.

Origin response headers like `Surrogate-Control` are stripped by AFD before reaching clients — use `Cache-Control: max-age` on origin for downstream browser caching in addition to AFD TTL.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Configure cache override on an AFD route
az afd route update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myAFDProfile \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --origin-group myOriginGroup \
  --route-name myRoute \
  --enable-caching true \
  --query-string-caching-behavior IgnoreQueryString \
  --forwarded-protocol HttpsOnly

# Purge specific paths after a deployment
az afd endpoint purge \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myAFDProfile \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --content-paths /api/config /assets/manifest.json

bytes.topic: Serverless — cold starts, timeout limits, stateless design constraints
bytes.title: Azure Functions Cold Starts — What Actually Happens and How to Fight Back
bytes.body:
On the Consumption plan, Azure Functions spin down after ~5 minutes of inactivity. The next invocation triggers a cold start: runtime init, package loading, and your app startup code all run before your function handler executes. For .NET isolated workers, this can hit 3–8s. HTTP-triggered functions feel this hardest since users are waiting synchronously.

Mitigations: Use the **Premium plan** with Always Ready instances (minimum 1 pre-warmed instance, no cold start). For Consumption, keep startup code lean — register singletons outside the handler, not inside. Azure's `WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1` reduces cold start by mounting a read-only zip rather than extracting files.

Timeout limits matter too: Consumption caps execution at **10 minutes**, Premium/Dedicated at **unlimited** (host.json default is 30 min). Design stateless — never rely on in-memory state between invocations. Use Durable Functions (`IDurableOrchestrationContext`) for stateful workflows; it externalizes state to Azure Storage automatically, surviving restarts and scale-out across multiple instances.

bytes.topic: Edge Computing — Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, latency benefits
bytes.title: Azure Edge Compute vs Cloudflare Workers — What Azure Front Door Actually Gives You
bytes.body: Azure Front Door Premium spans 192+ PoPs globally and handles TLS termination, anycast routing, and WAF at the edge — but it doesn't execute arbitrary JavaScript like Cloudflare Workers or Vercel Edge Functions. Azure's edge logic lives in the Rules Engine: header rewrites, URL redirects, and geo-based origin selection with zero cold starts. For actual code execution, Azure Static Web Apps links to Azure Functions, but those run regionally, not at CDN PoPs — you still pay a regional round-trip. The real TTFB win on Azure comes from combining Front Door's anycast entry with aggressive Azure CDN caching (Akamai or Verizon tiers), collapsing global TTFB to ~30–60ms. If you need V8 isolate-style edge workers today, the pragmatic pattern is Cloudflare Workers fronting your Azure Container App origin: geo routing and A/B logic happen at CF's edge, while Azure handles stateful compute and data. This hybrid avoids duplicating auth infrastructure while still hitting sub-10ms edge decisions.

bytes.topic: Managed Services — when managed beats self-hosted (Kafka, Redis, Postgres)
bytes.title: Azure Managed Services — When Event Hubs, Cache for Redis, and Flexible Server Beat Self-Hosted
bytes.body:
Self-hosting Kafka, Redis, or Postgres on Azure VMs means owning OS patches, disk IOPS tuning, HA failover scripts, and backup windows. Azure's managed equivalents eliminate that toil — with tradeoffs worth knowing.

Azure Event Hubs exposes a Kafka-compatible endpoint (`<namespace>.servicebus.windows.net:9093`). You lose custom broker configs and Kafka Streams, but gain auto-scale throughput units, Geo-DR via paired namespaces, and Capture directly to ADLS Gen2 with zero custom connectors.

Azure Cache for Redis (Enterprise tier) runs Redis 7.x with Active Geo-Replication and first-class support for RediSearch and RedisJSON modules. Self-hosted wins only if you need RESP3 or unsupported community modules.

Azure Database for PostgreSQL — Flexible Server ships with pgvector, logical replication slots, and built-in PgBouncer connection pooling. Setting `high_availability.mode = ZoneRedundant` delivers sub-30-second automatic failover — painful to replicate on raw VMs.

Choose managed when SLA accountability, compliance (SOC 2, ISO 27001 included), and operational toil cost more than the loss of deep configurability.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Provision all three managed services via Azure CLI

# Event Hubs with Kafka protocol enabled
az eventhubs namespace create \
  --name myns --resource-group rg-prod \
  --sku Standard --enable-kafka true --location eastus

# Redis Enterprise with Active Geo-Replication support
az redisenterprise create \
  --name myredis --resource-group rg-prod \
  --sku EnterpriseFlash_F300 --location eastus

# PostgreSQL Flexible Server with Zone-Redundant HA
az postgres flexible-server create \
  --name mypg --resource-group rg-prod \
  --sku-name Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --high-availability ZoneRedundant --location eastus

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, secrets manager, WAF, DDoS protection
bytes.title: Azure Security Hardening — Encryption, Key Vault, WAF, and DDoS in One Stack
bytes.body:
Azure encrypts data at rest by default using platform-managed keys, but production workloads should use Customer-Managed Keys (CMK) in Azure Key Vault with a dedicated HSM-backed tier. For Blob Storage, enable CMK via `Set-AzStorageAccount -KeyvaultEncryption`. In transit, enforce TLS 1.2+ on App Service via `minTlsVersion: '1.2'` in your Bicep/ARM template — never rely on defaults.

Secrets management belongs exclusively in Key Vault. Use managed identity references (`@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=...)`) in App Service config instead of storing secrets directly — zero credential exposure in env vars or code.

For WAF, deploy Azure Front Door Premium or Application Gateway v2 with WAF Policy in Prevention mode. Attach OWASP 3.2 ruleset and add custom rules to block geo-regions or rate-limit by IP. Front Door gives you global anycast edge enforcement.

DDoS Protection Standard (now Microsoft Defender for DDoS) attaches to a Virtual Network and auto-tunes baselines per public IP. Pair it with Azure Monitor alerts on `DDoSAttackBytesForwarded` to trigger auto-scaling or firewall rule pushes via Logic Apps.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Assign CMK to Azure Storage using Key Vault
az storage account update \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --encryption-key-source Microsoft.Keyvault \
  --encryption-key-vault https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net \
  --encryption-key-name myKey \
  --encryption-key-version "" \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned

# Enforce TLS 1.2 minimum on App Service
az webapp config set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myApp \
  --min-tls-version 1.2

bytes.topic: PaaS vs IaaS — Railway/Vercel/Fly.io vs raw AWS/GCP/Azure, DX vs control
bytes.title: Azure Container Apps vs AKS — When PaaS DX Beats IaaS Control
bytes.body:
Azure spans the full spectrum from Railway-style push-and-go to raw infrastructure ownership. Azure Container Apps is Azure's direct competitor to Fly.io and Railway — deploy a container image from ACR, get HTTPS termination, Dapr integration, KEDA-powered autoscaling, and scale-to-zero on the consumption plan with zero Kubernetes YAML. Azure App Service plays the Vercel role for web workloads: deployment slots, custom domains, managed TLS, and Linux/Windows runtime stacks in under five minutes.

The ceiling appears fast. Container Apps locks you into Envoy as the ingress proxy; you can't swap CNI plugins or configure node-level sysctls. VNet integration requires the Workload Profiles plan, not the default consumption tier. Cold starts on scale-from-zero can hit 2–4 seconds under bursty traffic.

AKS is the IaaS escape hatch. You control node pool SKUs, cluster autoscaler profiles, custom NSG rules, and the full Kubernetes API surface — including GPU workloads, PodDisruptionBudgets, and Calico network policies. The cost: you own upgrade windows, node image patches, and ingress controller configuration.

Default to Container Apps. Escalate to AKS when VNet segmentation, GPU node pools, or fine-grained pod scheduling become non-negotiable.
bytes.code_snippet:
# PaaS: Azure Container Apps — production-ready in one command
az containerapp create \
  --name my-api \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --environment my-env \
  --image myacr.azurecr.io/api:latest \
  --target-port 8080 \
  --ingress external \
  --min-replicas 0 --max-replicas 10

# IaaS: AKS — cluster is just the start
az aks create --name my-cluster --resource-group rg-prod \
  --node-count 3 --node-vm-size Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --network-plugin azure --enable-addons monitoring
# Still need: kubectl apply for ingress, cert-manager, HPA, PDB, Dapr...

bytes.topic: Region Selection — latency, data residency, compliance requirements
bytes.title: Azure Region Selection — Latency, Data Residency, and Compliance
bytes.body: Picking an Azure region isn't just proximity to users — it's a three-way constraint: latency, data residency, and regulatory compliance. Use Azure Speed Test or the `az network watcher` latency report to measure round-trip times before committing. For data residency, Azure Geography boundaries (e.g., "Europe" encompasses North/West Europe) guarantee data never leaves the geo — critical for GDPR. Sovereign clouds (Azure Government, Azure China 21Vianet) are separate endpoints entirely, not just regions. Compliance requirements like PCI-DSS or HIPAA map to specific region capabilities — not every service is available in every region. Use `az account list-locations --query "[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}"` to enumerate, then cross-reference the Azure Products by Region page. For multi-region active-active, pair regions within the same geography (e.g., East US + West US 2) to use Azure Traffic Manager with priority or weighted routing. Availability Zones within a region handle fault isolation; paired regions handle disaster recovery. Always check `az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Sql --query "resourceTypes[].locations"` to confirm your required services exist in the target region before designing your architecture.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Check service availability in a specific region before committing
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Sql \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='servers'].locations" \
  --output tsv

# List regions with latency metadata
az network watcher list-available-providers \
  --location eastus \
  --query "countries[].states[].cities[].providers" \
  --output table

# Enumerate all regions in a geography
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?metadata.geographyGroup=='US'].{Name:name,Lat:metadata.latitude,Long:metadata.longitude}" \
  --output table

bytes.topic: Compliance — SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA considerations per provider
bytes.title: Azure Compliance — SOC 2, GDPR, and HIPAA Without the Guesswork
bytes.body:
Azure ships built-in Policy initiatives for all three frameworks. For HIPAA/HITRUST, assign the initiative ID `a169a624-5599-4385-a696-c8d643089fab` at subscription scope — it maps 300+ controls to your resources automatically. Microsoft Defender for Cloud's Regulatory Compliance dashboard then shows you a live control-by-control posture with remediation steps, no manual gap analysis needed.

For GDPR, enable Diagnostic Settings on every resource and route logs to a Log Analytics Workspace with a 90-day minimum retention. Use Azure Purview to classify sensitive data in Blob Storage and SQL — it detects PII columns automatically and feeds into your Data Subject Request (DSR) workflow.

HIPAA requires a signed BAA with Microsoft (done via the Microsoft Services Agreement portal) and Customer Lockbox enabled so Microsoft engineers can't access your data without explicit approval. Store audit logs in Azure Blob Storage with immutability policies (WORM) — set a time-based retention lock so logs can't be deleted or tampered with, satisfying both SOC 2 CC6.1 and HIPAA §164.312(b).
bytes.code_snippet:
# Assign HIPAA/HITRUST built-in initiative to a subscription
az policy assignment create \
  --name "hipaa-hitrust" \
  --display-name "HIPAA HITRUST 9.2" \
  --policy-set-definition "a169a624-5599-4385-a696-c8d643089fab" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --sku standard

# Enable WORM immutability on audit log container
az storage container immutability-policy create \
  --account-name <storage-account> \
  --container-name audit-logs \
  --period 2557

bytes.topic: Deployment Models — blue/green, canary, rolling updates on cloud infra
bytes.title: Azure Deployment Models — Blue/Green Slots, Canary Revisions, and Rolling Updates in AKS
bytes.body: Azure offers three native deployment patterns tied to specific compute planes. App Service deployment slots are the canonical blue/green tool: deploy to a staging slot, warm it up, then swap with `az webapp deployment slot swap`. The swap is a DNS pointer flip within the same App Service Plan — no cold starts, instant rollback by swapping back. Azure Container Apps uses revision-based traffic splitting for canary: each deploy creates a new revision, and `az containerapp ingress traffic set` lets you shift weights incrementally (e.g., 95/5 → 80/20 → 0/100). Dapr sidecars and scale rules are revision-scoped, so each canary cohort is fully isolated. AKS handles rolling updates natively via `strategy.rollingUpdate`, but tune `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` per workload criticality, and pair with `PodDisruptionBudgets` to cap simultaneous evictions. Gate all three with Azure Load Testing on the inactive revision or slot before shifting production weight, and use Application Insights availability tests with Azure Monitor action groups to trigger automated rollbacks on latency or error-rate breaches.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy a new Container Apps revision and start a 10% canary split
az containerapp update \
  --name myapp \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --revision-suffix v2 \
  --image myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:v2

az containerapp ingress traffic set \
  --name myapp \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --revision-weight myapp--v1=90 myapp--v2=10

# Promote to 100% once metrics are clean
az containerapp ingress traffic set \
  --name myapp \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --revision-weight myapp--v2=100

bytes.topic: Multi-cloud & Vendor Lock-in — portability trade-offs, abstraction layers
bytes.title: Azure Vendor Lock-in — Where Dapr Buys You Portability Without the Tax
bytes.body:
Azure's managed services — Service Bus, Cosmos DB, Azure Functions triggers — offer elite DX but embed portability debt. The real lock-in isn't egress fees; it's cognitive: your team internalizes Azure-specific SDKs, ARM resource models, and Entra IAM patterns that don't transfer anywhere else.

Dapr (Distributed Application Runtime) is the most practical abstraction layer for Azure workloads. Its pub/sub, state store, and secrets building blocks sit atop Azure Service Bus, Cosmos DB, and Azure Key Vault respectively — wired via component YAML files. Swap the YAML, not the application code.

Trade-off: Dapr adds ~2ms sidecar latency per call and requires AKS or Azure Container Apps with sidecar injection enabled. For throughput-critical paths (e.g., >10K msg/s on Service Bus Premium), the native SDK wins by a margin that matters.

For IaC, Terraform beats Bicep on portability. Bicep compiles to ARM — first-class and fast, but Azure-only. Terraform's azurerm provider uses the same HCL as your AWS and GCP modules, letting one platform team own multi-cloud infra without context-switching.

Pick lock-in deliberately: go native where performance and DX justify it, abstract at the seams where migration risk is real.

bytes.topic: Monitoring & Billing — cost anomaly detection, usage dashboards
bytes.title: Azure Cost Anomaly Detection — Catch Spend Spikes Before They Hit Your Budget
bytes.body:
Azure Cost Management's anomaly detection runs daily ML models over your subscription spend and fires alerts when usage deviates from baseline. Enable it via Cost Analysis → Anomaly Alerts or programmatically through the Cost Management REST API. Pair this with Azure Monitor Action Groups to route anomaly notifications to Slack, PagerDuty, or email without polling.

For dashboards, the `Microsoft.CostManagement/query` API lets you pull granular usage data into Azure Managed Grafana or Power BI. Use the `ActualCost` dataset with a `grouping` by `ServiceName` and `ResourceGroupName` to surface which workloads are drifting. Set budget alerts at 80% and 100% thresholds via `Microsoft.Consumption/budgets` — these are distinct from anomaly alerts and fire on absolute spend, not statistical deviation.

For multi-subscription environments, scope queries to a Management Group and use `BillingAccountId` as the grouping key. Export raw usage daily to a Storage Account via Scheduled Exports for long-term trend analysis with Azure Data Explorer or Synapse.
bytes.code_snippet:
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/{sub-id}" \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"PreTaxCost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ServiceName"},{"type":"Dimension","name":"ResourceGroupName"}]' \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --query 'properties.rows' \
  --output table
